title: Present Perfect – for and since II

questions.question: He hasn't called me ___ weeks.
It hasn't rained here ___ March.
I've had this phone ___ two years.
They've lived next door ___ we moved in.
She's been studying medicine ___ four years.
I haven't been to the cinema ___ Christmas.
He's been waiting in the queue ___ an hour.
We haven't spoken ___ the wedding.
She's been a nurse ___ fifteen years.
He has played guitar ___ he was a teenager.
They've been on holiday ___ a week.
I've been feeling unwell ___ yesterday.
She hasn't eaten anything ___ two days.
He hasn't visited his parents ___ last summer.
We've been together ___ three years.
I have known him ___ we were at school.
She's been off work ___ a month.
They haven't been in touch ___ the funeral.
He's been saving money ___ years.
I've felt much better ___ I started exercising.

theory.explanation:
FOR and SINCE in Present Perfect

We use both with Present Perfect to describe how long something has lasted.

FOR + length of time (how long?):
  for two hours, for ten years, for a long time, for ages

SINCE + a point in time (since when?):
  since Monday, since 2010, since I was a child, since breakfast

Tip: ask yourself:
  "How long?" → FOR
  "Since when?" → SINCE

examples.sentence: He hasn't called me for weeks.
examples.explanation: FOR + period (weeks). How long since he called?

examples.sentence: It hasn't rained here since March.
examples.explanation: SINCE + point in time (March). Since when has it not rained?

examples.sentence: I have known him since we were at school.
examples.explanation: SINCE + subordinate clause (since + subject + verb). Since when have I known him?
